coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of gradient band tin oxide electron transport layer using self-separated dual-quantum dots for perovskite solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yang
Li, Xiabing
Chen, Bo
Tang, Yuan
Wang, Jun
Lu, Hao
Guo, Chunxian
Tong, Shuk-Yin
coredata.dc:description: Constructing a gradient band structure electron transport layer (ETL) and thereby enhancing the performance and stability of perovskite solar cells are an effective approach. Leveraging two types of quantum dots (QDs) with different masses, namely GQDs and Au QDs, along with tin oxide (SnO2) colloidal particles, we successfully prepared an ETL with a gradient band structure. Perovskite solar cells fabricated based on this gradient band ETL achieved a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 22.2 %, whereas those prepared with a conventional SnO2 ETL yielded only 19.6 % efficiency. Furthermore, under continuous AM 1.5 G and 45 °C temperature illumination for 1000 h, the cells retained 91 % of their initial efficiency. Further investigation revealed that the construction of this gradient band structure effectively compensated for the defects of the SnO2, thereby improving its electrical properties. Additionally, it enhanced the transport and separate ability of charge carriers in the ETL, reducing recombination probabilities. This work provides a simple and effective method for constructing gradient band structures, which not only enhances the performance of perovskite solar cells but also holds promise for other photovoltaic conversion devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite solar cell
Graded energy band
Tin oxide
Graphene quantum dots
Gold quantum dots

We would like to thank Jun Wang for his assistance in revision process and Xiaoyan Wang for her assistance in polishing the language of the manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (a) GQDs, (b) SnO2-GQDs and (c) SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs samples; (d) Top-view SEM and (e) EDAX mapping of SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL; (f) Au XPS spectra of SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL. EDAX: energy-dispersive X-ray; ETL: electron transport layer; GQD: graphene quantum dots; QDs: quantum dots; SEM: scanning electron microscope; XPS: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy

The contact angle test of (a) SnO2, (b) SnO2-GQDs and (c) SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs samples; (d) UV–vis band gap spectra and (e) UPS spectra of different ETLs; (f) Band gap diagram of different ETLs. ETL: electron transport layer; GQDs: graphene quantum dots

Top-view SEM of perovskite layer on (a) SnO2, (b) SnO2-GQDs and (c) SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs samples; (d) PbI2 rate of perovskite layer on different ETLs; (e) XRD spectra of different ETLs; (f) Absorption test of perovskite layer on different ETLs. ETLs: electron transport layers; GQDs: graphene quantum dots; QDs: quantum dots; SEM: scanning electron microscope; XRD: X-ray diffraction

(a) Cross-sectional SEM image of perovskite solar cell based by SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL; (b) J-V curves of perovskite solar cells on different ETLs; (c) PL test, and (d) SPV test of perovskite layer on different ETLs; (e) leakage current test and (f) EQE spectra of perovskite solar cells on different ETLs. ETLs: electron transport layers; GQDs: graphene quantum dots; PL: photoluminescence; SEM: scanning electron microscope; SPV: surface photovoltage

(a) M−S test and (b) SCLC test of perovskite solar cells on different ETLs; (c) light intensity-dependent V
OC of perovskite solar cells on different ETLs; (d) Hysteresis measurement of perovskite solar cells on different ETLs; (e) J
SC distribution and (f) efficiency distribution histogram of perovskite solar cells on different ETLs. ETLs: electron transport layers; SCLC: space charge–limited current; V
OC : open-circuit voltage; J
SC : short-circuit current

(a) Schematic diagram of QDs doping perovskite solar cell; (b) etched UPS spectra of SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL; (c) Schematic diagram of gradient bandgap; (d) MPP measurement of perovskite solar cell based on SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL; (e) Long-time test under AM 1.5 G 45 °C of perovskite solar cells on different ETLs. ETL: electron transport layer; MPP: maximum power point; QDs: quantum dots; UPS: ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy
The photovoltaic characteristic parameters of different samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
HI: hysteresis factor, PCE: power conversion efficiency; V
OC : open-circuit voltage; J
SC : short-circuit current; FF: fill factor

head.ce:title: Fabrication of gradient band tin oxide electron transport layer using self-separated dual-quantum dots for perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Science & Devices, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou, 215009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Suzhou University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Materials Science & Devices, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou, 215009, China

ce:footnote.ce:label: a
ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Constructing a gradient band structure electron transport layer (ETL) and thereby enhancing the performance and stability of perovskite solar cells are an effective approach. Leveraging two types of quantum dots (QDs) with different masses, namely GQDs and Au QDs, along with tin oxide (SnO2) colloidal particles, we successfully prepared an ETL with a gradient band structure. Perovskite solar cells fabricated based on this gradient band ETL achieved a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 22.2 %, whereas those prepared with a conventional SnO2 ETL yielded only 19.6 % efficiency. Furthermore, under continuous AM 1.5 G and 45 °C temperature illumination for 1000 h, the cells retained 91 % of their initial efficiency. Further investigation revealed that the construction of this gradient band structure effectively compensated for the defects of the SnO2, thereby improving its electrical properties. Additionally, it enhanced the transport and separate ability of charge carriers in the ETL, reducing recombination probabilities. This work provides a simple and effective method for constructing gradient band structures, which not only enhances the performance of perovskite solar cells but also holds promise for other photovoltaic conversion devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cell has garnered considerable attention within the photovoltaic field in recent years due to its adjusting bandgap, effective light absorbance ability, and low cost [1–3]. The power conversion efficiency of perovskite solar cell has increased from 3.8 % to more than 26 % within less than twenty years [4–6]. In addition to its excellent optoelectronic conversion performance, the simplicity of preparation for perovskite solar cell is also a significant factor contributing to the rapid development of perovskite devices [7–9]. Whether in planar devices or mesoporous devices, most perovskite solar cells generally consist of five layers: conductive glass, electron transport layer (ETL), perovskite layer, hole transport layer (HTL), and top electrode [10,11]. The ETL, which transfers photogenerated charge carriers from the perovskite layer to the conductive glass, plays a crucial role in the solar cells [12–14]. The quality of its charge carrier transport greatly affects the device's optoelectronic conversion efficiency.

Metal oxides such as titanium dioxide (TiO2), zinc oxide (ZnO), and tin oxide (SnO2) have been important constituents of the ETL in perovskite solar cell development from its inception to the present day [15–17]. Among these materials, SnO2 has garnered significant attention due to its excellent electrical conductivity, good chemical stability, and straightforward fabrication methods compared with others [18,19]. You et al. utilizing commercial SnO2 nanoparticles with a simple spin-coating method to fabricate the SnO2 ETL [20]. This method offers simplicity in fabrication and relatively high efficiency, thereby attracting widespread interest. Subsequently, numerous papers have been published on SnO2 nanoparticles [21–23]. However, the efficiency of devices using single SnO2 nanoparticles remains limited due to surface defects. Effective modification methods to reduce the defects of SnO2 nanoparticles and thereby fabricate perovskite solar cells represent a promising approach to achieving high performance and good stable devices [24,25]. Chemical modification is an effective method for passivating defects and enhancing device performance, but experimental procedures can inevitably be complex. It may be feasible to explore using materials with similar particle sizes to SnO2 nanoparticles and employing doping methods to enhance device performance without altering the original straightforward fabrication process. This approach holds significant research value as it offers the potential to improve device performance while maintaining simplicity in fabrication techniques.

Quantum dot (QD) materials offer excellent size tunability, making them potentially effective dopants. Their utilization in perovskite solar cells is a current research hotspot [26–28]. By combining the superior properties of two-dimensional QDs and metal QDs and integrating them with a solution of SnO2 nanoparticles, it may be possible to fabricate high-performance ETL using a simple spin-coating method. This approach holds promise as an effective means of producing high-quality perovskite solar cells. Zhang et al. adopted a double-layer synergistic optimization approach by simultaneously introducing functional black phosphorus quantum dots (BPQDs) into the ETL and the perovskite layer, effectively filling electron traps and enhancing the electron mobility of SnO2 [29]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional nanometer-scale particles made of graphene, featuring high electrical conductivity and excellent chemical stability. Li et al. incorporated GQDs with efficient charge carrier transport capability into a tin oxide (SnO2) solution and utilized their distinct mass properties for effective self-stratification. By employing an antisolvent spin-coating technique, they prepared a tin oxide electron transport layer (ETL) with a gradient energy band structure [30]. Gold quantum dots (Au QDs) demonstrate pronounced surface plasmon resonance effects, which result in strong light absorption and scattering at specific wavelengths, thus improving the light absorption efficiency of the perovskite layer. While Au QDs improve light absorption, GQDs enhance charge transport. Combining both types of quantum dots can significantly reduce charge recombination and improve the overall efficiency of perovskite solar cells [31].

Here, by introducing GQDs and Au QDs into a solution of SnO2 nanoparticles, we fabricated SnO2 ETL with a gradient band structure through self-assembly during spin coating due to the different weights of the materials. After optimization, perovskite solar cells prepared with Au-GQDs-SnO2 hybrid ETL achieved an efficiency of 22.2 %, whereas cells prepared with the comparative single SnO2 ETL only reached 19.6 % efficiency. Furthermore, the devices modified after long-term stability testing under standard solar illumination AM 1.5G retained 91 % efficiency, while the unmodified comparative devices only maintained 70 % efficiency. Through our research, we found that the introduction of GQDs and Au QDs effectively passivated defects in the SnO2 ETL, thereby improving the charge carrier transport capacity of the ETL. Additionally, the perovskite layer grown on the quantum dot-modified ETL exhibited larger grain size and fewer defects. This simple experimental approach not only enables the fabrication of high-performance gradient band ETLs for perovskite solar cells but also opens new avenues for applications in other optoelectronic devices.

The conductive glass (indium tin oxide, ITO) was cleaned by ultrasonic vibration for 25 min with ethanol mixed with glass cleaning solution and deionized water. The treated ITO base was placed into the ozone machine and treated for 30 min. SnO2 (Alfa Aesar, 15 % in H2O colloidal dispersion) was diluted with deionized water at a ratio of 1:7, and ultrasonic oscillation was performed for 30 min. The GQDs were used after bought from Suzhou Tanfeng Graphene Technology Co., Ltd (0.3–2.0 % mass ratio, and all weight % are based on total solution). The Au QDs were obtained by dissolving chloroauric acid in an aqueous solution, followed by natural hydrolysis (1.0–1.4 mM). The SnO2-GQDs ETLs was prepared by mixing 75 μL of GQDs, 100 μL of SnO2 solution, and 625 μL of deionized water, followed by sonication for 25 min. The SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs electron transport layer (ETL) was obtained by mixing 100 μL of diluted Au solution, 75 μL of GQDs, 100 μL of SnO2 solution, and 525 μL of deionized water.
The ETLs were prepared by spin-coating the solution to ITO at a spin speed of 5000 rpm for 30 s. Then the substrate was annealed in air at 150 °C for 30 min.

Perovskite solar cells were prepared by one step method. The 736 mg PbI2, 224 mg formamidine hydroiodide (FAI), 16.2 mg MABr, 19.8 mg CsI, and 20.0 mg MACl were dissolved in 800 μL N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and 200 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). Before use, the solution was filtered using a filter head, and then 50 μL of the solution was uniformly spread onto the ETL. During the spin-coating process, anti-solvent was dropwise added to the sample to improve the film quality. Specifically, 200 μL of anti-solvent was quickly added during the 10 s of spinning, followed by another 20-s spin, resulting in a black perovskite film. The sample was then transferred to a hot plate and heated at 150 °C for 10 min to obtain a high-quality perovskite film. Then, 50 μL Spiro-OMeTAD which was dissolved in 1 ml chlorobenzene with 39 μL tri-butyl-phosphate (TBP), 23 μL LiTFSI, and 5 μL Tris(2-(1H-pyrazol-1-yl)-4-tert-butylpyridine) (FK) 209 Co(III) tris(bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide) (TFSI) salt was doped on the top perovskite layer. The HTL was spin-coated with 2000 rpm. for 30 s. Finally, a 100 nm thick Ag was deposited by thermal evaporation with a shadow mask (0.15 cm2 active area). And for long-time tested sample, a 50 nm thick Au was deposited by the same way. The prepared sample would be placed in the oxidation chamber for 12 h before the test.

The morphologies of the samples were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai, G20) and scanning electron microscopy (JEOL, JSM-IT800). The films were investigated by X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, D8 Advance). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, ESCALAB 250Xi). The Photoluminescence were tested with 475 nm laser (Edinburgh Instruments, LP320). The contact angle measurement was tested by DSA25E (KRÜSS). The absorbance and transmission measurement were tested by UV-2600 (Shimadzu). The transient surface photovoltage was measured by the transient surface photovoltage detection system (Perfectlight, PL-TPV) and decay was recorded on a digital oscilloscope (Tektronix, MS054). The photovoltaic parameters of solar cells were measured under Newport solar simulator AM 1.5G irradiation (100 mW/cm2) with a Keithley 2400, and external quantum efficiency (EQE) curves were characterized by Zolix system. J-V test was prepared with dwell time of 500 ms, scan step of 0.04 V, scan rate of 100 mV/s. Before the cell test, calibrate the sunlight through the simulator, and the certificate number of the calibrated Si cell is GXtc2017-2581. The Mott–Schottky and space charge–limited current were measured with Autolab (PGSTAT 302 N) in glove box. Light intensity-dependent open-circuit voltage (VOC ) was measured under AM 1.5G with different filters under the test fixture.

The state and size of nanoparticles have a significant impact on their aggregation when combined. To investigate this, we conducted tests on the samples using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In Fig. 1 a, clear lattice fringes belonging to GQDs nanoparticles are visible, with grain sizes within 10 nm. Fig. 1b shows the TEM image of the sample containing a mixture of SnO2 nanoparticles and GQDs, where both SnO2 and GQDs' lattices are discernible, with GQD sizes also within 10 nm. In Fig. 1c, the sample contains a mixture of Au QDs, GQDs, and SnO2, and all three exhibit clear lattice fringes with similar sizes. This similarity in size facilitates the combination of the three components without significant aggregation, aiding in the formation of a compact ETL. Moreover, these clear lattice fringes indicate that these materials possess good crystalline properties with fewer defects, which are favorable for charge carrier transport. We also conducted UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis.) tests on the mixed solution. In Fig. S1, distinct absorption peaks corresponding to Au QDs and a small characteristic absorption peak for GQDs were observed. These findings indicate that both types of nanoparticles have relatively uniform sizes. To examine whether these small absorptions would affect the transparency of the ETL, thus reducing light absorption by the perovskite layer, we performed transmittance tests on different ETLs. From Fig. S2, the added nanoparticles do not decrease light transmittance. Considering the plasmonic resonance effect of gold, the ETL with added Au QDs exhibits even better transparency in the 400–550 nm range. Fig. 1d shows a scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of the ETL prepared using the mixed sample. It can be observed that we have achieved a compact ETL without pin-holes in the mixed ETL. Additionally, the sample retains the morphology of the conductive glass, indicating a relatively thin thickness of this ETL. SEM images of the ITO and ETLs prepared using single SnO2, GQDs-SnO2 mixed solution can be found in Fig. S3, which show similar morphologies without pin-holes. To assess whether the three components are uniformly distributed in the ETL, we conducted elemental surface scanning analysis on the sample. From the elemental scans of blue for Au, red for C, green for Sn, and yellow for O shown in Fig. 1e, it is evident that all three are evenly distributed on the ETL. To further explore the state of Au nanoparticles, we conducted X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) tests on the sample, and the test results and fitted peaks are shown in Fig. 1f. It can be observed that we effectively fitted two peaks belonging to the Au 4f orbitals, indicating the presence of Au in the ETL [32]. Additionally, we compared the sample with single SnO2, and from Fig. S4, there is no Au peak information at this position in single SnO2 sample.

The hydrophilicity of the ETL plays a crucial role in the subsequent spin-coating of the perovskite layer. We conducted contact angle tests on the different ETL samples. Fig. 2 shows the contact angle test results for single SnO2, SnO2-GQDs, and SnO2-GQDs-Au ETL samples. From the results, it can be observed that the SnO2-GQDs-Au sample has the smallest contact angle, indicating best hydrophilicity. The contact angle of the SnO2-GQDs sample is similar to that of the single SnO2 sample, suggesting that the improvement in hydrophilicity of the SnO2-GQDs-Au sample may be attributed to the small amount of added Au particles. To investigate the band structure changes in different ETLs, we analyzed the samples using UV–vis. and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). From Fig. 2d, it can be observed that the bandgap width of the single SnO2 sample is 3.41 eV. After doping with GQDs and Au QDs, the bandgap width of the mixed SnO2 ETL increased to 3.48 eV. This may be attributed to the introduction of highly conductive materials, which alters the electron cloud state of SnO2. To determine the state of the SnO2, we conducted XPS tests on the different ETLs. From Fig. S5, it can be observed that there is no significant change in the Sn electron orbitals among the three ETLs. This indicates that the doping of QDs did not cause an obvious change in the state of the Sn element. However, for the O element, it is evident that with the addition of QDs, there is a noticeable decrease in the peak corresponding to oxygen vacancies. This suggests that the introduction of QDs effectively modifies the concentration of oxygen vacancies in SnO2, thereby improving the performance of the ETL. Combining with the UPS results shown in Fig. 2e, the Fermi energies are extracted from the cutoff region by Ef = 21.2 eV (He I) - Ecutoff [33], we can calculate the Ef of these three ETLs to be 4.93 eV for single SnO2, 4.90 eV for SnO2-GQDs, and 4.83 eV for SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs, respectively. The Fermi level of the single SnO2 sample is the lowest, while that of the SnO2-GQDs sample is in the middle, and the sample with mixed GQDs and Au QDs has the highest Fermi level. Considering that SnO2 is a typical n-type semiconductor, this increase in the Fermi level indicates that the addition of QDs effectively introduces more free electrons. These additional electrons can occupy defect states in SnO2, thereby reducing the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers in the ETL and improving the performance of perovskite solar cells. The UPS full spectra information for these three sets of samples can be observed in Fig. S6. Fig. 2f illustrates the calculated band structures of the three ETLs. The valence band (EV) of the three ETLs, calculated using the equation EV = 21.2 - (Ecutoff - Ef), are 7.98 eV, 8.21 eV, and 8.51 eV, respectively. Further, using conduction band (EC) = EV + Eg, the conduction band of each electron layer are determined. The test results indicate that the addition of mixed QDs effectively improves the hydrophilicity and can also enhance the electrical properties of the ETL, thereby facilitating the preparation of subsequent perovskite layers and the transport of charge carriers.

Fig. 3 depicts the morphology of the perovskite films on different ETLs, with insets showing the distribution of perovskite grain sizes in Fig. S7. Fig. 3a shows the perovskite layer prepared on a single SnO2 ETL, Fig. 3b shows the perovskite layer prepared on SnO2-GQDs ETL, and Fig. 3c shows the perovskite layer prepared on SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL. From the SEM images, it can be observed that all three perovskite layers exhibit good compact, but the perovskite layer on the single SnO2 ETL contains many small white grains. These white grains are generally considered to be lead iodide (PbI2), and the presence of PbI2 can degrade the stability of the perovskite layer and reduce device efficiency. In contrast, there are no obvious white grains on the perovskite layers on QDs-doped ETLs. Additionally, from the grain size distribution plots, samples with QDs doping exhibit larger grain sizes. This larger grain size can effectively reduce the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers at grain boundaries, thereby improving device performance. To better compare the perovskite grains with PbI2 grains, we plotted the ratio of perovskite to PbI2 with a red curve and the number of PbI2 with a blue curve. From Fig. 3d, the ratio of perovskite to PbI2 increased from 0.71 on the single SnO2 sample to 1.6 on the SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs sample. From this ratio, it is evident that there is an increase in perovskite grains. Additionally, the grains number of PbI2 also indicates a decrease in the amount from 54 to 26. We also utilized X-ray diffraction to analyze the quality of the perovskite films. From Fig. 3e, it can be observed that there is no significant change in the perovskite film due to the addition of QDs. The characteristic peaks of the perovskite (110) plane remain very clear, while there is a slight weakening of the characteristic peaks of PbI2 [34]. The X-ray diffraction spectra of the perovskite layers on ETLs with different GQDs and Au QDs doping are shown in Fig. S8 and Fig. S9. From the figures, it can be observed that varying amounts of QDs do not have a significant effect on the perovskite films. The perovskite films still exhibit high quality. Fig. 3f displays the absorption spectra of the perovskite films deposited on these three ETLs. All perovskite films exhibit similar absorption edges close to 800 nm, which matches their bandgap. Additionally, the perovskite layer prepared on the SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL shows the highest absorption, which correlates with the transmittance measurement. The test results indicate that by utilizing QDs doping, we can fabricate perovskite layers with higher quality.

Fig. 4 a illustrates the cross-sectional information of the perovskite solar cell based on SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL. From the figure, it can be observed that each layer of the cell maintains excellent smoothness and is compact within a 3 μm scan bar. Upon closer inspection, it is evident that perovskite grains penetrate both the ETL and the HTL, exhibiting excellent compactness in both regions. This indicates the fabrication of a high-quality perovskite layer. Cross-sectional images of samples based on single SnO2 and SnO2-GQDs can be found in Fig. S10, where the perovskite layer still maintains good density, but perovskite grains do not penetrate through both transport layers. Fig. 4b displays the current density-voltage (J-V) testing curves of the perovskite solar cells. Detailed electrical characteristic parameters, such as open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current (J
SC ), fill factor, and power conversion efficiency , are provided in Table 1 . From the graph, it can be observed that the sample based on SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs exhibits the highest J
SC of 24.7 mA/cm2, the highest V
OC of 1.14 V, and get an efficiency of 22.2 %, while single SnO2 sample only gets an efficiency of 19.8 %. The highest J
SC observed in the SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs sample is likely due to its superior optical absorption capability, while the highest V
OC is attributed to its highest Fermi level. To further explore the influence of the ETLs on devices performance, we conducted photoluminescence (PL) tests on the samples. From Fig. 4c, it can be observed that the addition of QDs effectively enhances the ability of the ETL to transport charge carriers, resulting in the lowest peak intensity for the SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs sample, followed by the SnO2-GQDs sample. Subsequently, we performed transient surface photovoltage (SPV) tests on the samples. Fig. 4d shows that the SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs sample exhibits the fastest voltage decay, indicating the most effective separation and transfer of surface charges, which is consistent with our PL test results. Fig. 4e displays the dark leakage current test results, which measure the intrinsic charge carrier transport performance in the dark. It can be observed that the leakage current of the three ETLs approaches a minimum, indicating similar leakage resistance capabilities. Fig. 4f presents the EQE spectra, which indicate the sample's absorption strength for a single spectrum of light, and the theoretically integrated current should approximate the J
SC . It can be seen from the graph that samples based on the three ETLs exhibit similar external quantum efficiency responses. The SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs sample shows slightly higher integrated current density, which is consistent with the J-V test results. It is worth noting that our EQE tests were conducted in a test chamber with a quartz cover, which introduces nearly 10 % light reflection. Therefore, the EQE integral results may be slightly lower than the J
SC values obtained from the J-V tests.

Fig. 5 a depicts the Mott–Schottky (M−S) tests of different ETLs, where the intersection point of the curve with the zero line indicates the built-in potential (Vbi) of the samples. From the curves, it can be observed that the sample based on SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs exhibits the highest Vbi. The increased Vbi suggests that the sample can more effectively separate charge carriers, thereby enhancing the V
OC , which is also consistent with the V
OC results obtained from the J-V tests. To further investigate the internal trap states of the samples, we conducted space charge–limited current test. Fig. 5b shows that SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs sample has the lowest trap-filled limit voltage (V
TFL ) of 0.3 V, while the single SnO2 sample has the highest V
TFL of 0.41 V [35,36]. In Fig. S11, the electron conductivity of the SnO2 sample was measured to be 5.58
×
10−8 S/cm, while the SnO2-GQDs sample was measured to be 6.32
×
10−8 S/cm. The SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs sample has the highest electron conductivity of 6.77
×
10−8 S/cm. Higher electrical conductivity can facilitate the effective extraction of electrons in the device, thereby enhancing the rate of electron transport.

As observed from the SEM cross-sections, perovskite layers based on these three ETLs have approximately the same thickness. Therefore, this lowest V
TFL also indicates that the sample has the lowest trap density. The variation of V
OC values under different light intensities is also a good measure of the device's response to Shockley-Read-Hall recombination[ [37], 38]. From the values in Fig. 5c, the addition of QDs effectively reduces this recombination. The 1.43 and 1.77 kT/q for the ETLs with QDs are both lower than the 1.93 kT/q for the single SnO2 ETL. The nonoverlapping current curves of perovskite solar cells during forward and reverse sweeps are also a common issue in perovskite solar cells. We conducted forward and reverse sweep tests on the cells, and the specific values were calculated using (PCEReverse-PCEForward)/PCEReverse, and shown in Table 1. From the curve in Fig. 5d, the data for the forward and reverse scan curves are presented in Table S1. SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs have the lowest value of 0.063, while the single SnO2 sample has the highest value of 0.101. The repeatability of perovskite solar cells is also an important factor in assessing device performance. Fig. 5e shows the distribution statistics of J
SC for 30 samples. From the graph, samples based on SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL have the smallest distribution range, highest median, and mean values, indicating better repeatability and reproducibility for this ETL-based device. Fig. 5f displays the efficiency distribution histogram for 30 samples based on these three ETLs, showing that samples based on SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL have a higher efficiency distribution.

Fig. 6 a illustrates the schematic diagram of QDs doping. When QDs are doped into the SnO2 solution, they naturally stratify within the SnO2 film due to their different masses. The heavier Au tends to settle at the bottom, while the relatively lighter GQDs remain in the upper layer of the SnO2. Such a layered ETL also leads to a stratified change in the internal band structure. To analyze this, we utilized etching UPS to examine the samples. From the etching UPS test results shown in Fig. 6b, it is evident that the band positions of the samples undergo significant changes with etching time. Fig. 6c displays the specific numerical values of the band structure, along with a schematic diagram. It can be observed that we successfully constructed an ETL with a gradient band structure using these self-segregated QDs, which is also the reason why the device doped with dual QDs exhibits the best performance. We have conducted time-dependent XPS measurements in Fig. S12. By varying the etching time, we obtained information from different depths of the sample. Analysis of the spectra collected at varying etching times reveals a significant reduction in C content with increasing depth, while the Au content increases substantially with extended etching. We also conducted maximum power point (MPP) tracking tests on the devices, and after 600 s of testing, there was no significant decay in the SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL-based sample's performance. The MPP test results for the other two ETLs can be seen in Fig. S13, where after 600 s of testing, the performance of these two samples is slightly lower than the initial values. Subsequently, we performed long-term steady-state testing under AM 1.5 G and 45 °C, and it can be observed that when the efficiency of the SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs sample decreased to 91 % of the initial value, the efficiency of the GQDs sample dropped to 87 %, while the efficiency of the single SnO2 sample was only 70 % of the initial value.

In summary, through the doping of GQDs and Au QDs, we achieved modification of the SnO2 ETL and subsequently developed efficient and stable perovskite solar cells. The perovskite solar cells based on the SnO2-GQDs-Au QDs ETL achieved an efficiency of 22.2 %, and retained 91 % efficiency after 1000 h of simulated sunlight exposure without encapsulation, whereas the reference perovskite solar cell using single SnO2 ETL achieved only 19.8 % efficiency, with a decrease to 70 % efficiency after 1000 h of illumination. Further investigation revealed that the improvement in performance was attributed to the self-separation phenomenon of the dual QDs based on their different masses, which constructed a gradient band structure in the SnO2 ETL. This gradient band structure enhanced the charge carrier transport capability while reducing the defect density of the SnO2, effectively improving the electrical properties of the ETL. The successfully constructed gradient band ETL can not only enhance the performance and stability of perovskite solar cells but also find applications in other perovskite optoelectronic devices.

Yang Guo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiabing Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Bo Chen: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Yuan Tang: Writing – review & editing. Jun Wang: Writing – review & editing. Hao Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Chunxian Guo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Shuk-Yin Tong: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52472166, 22108187), Excellent Youth Foundation of Jiangsu Science Committee (BK20220118, BK20230073), Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (22KJD430009), Jiangsu Province Academician Workstation (Grant BM2022114). We would like to thank Jun Wang for his assistance in revision process and Xiaoyan Wang for her assistance in polishing the language of the manuscript.
